phrasal_verbs.meaning: reply rudely to someone who has more authority than you
invite someone to go with you to a cinema, restaurant, etc because you want to start a romantic or sexual relationship with them
if two people break up, they end their relationship; break something to make smaller pieces; if a meeting or other event breaks up, or if you break it up, it ends and people leave
make someone or something show a quality that they have; produce a new product and start to sell it
create a situation in which people meet and do something together, especially when they would not usually do so
meet someone unexpectedly; accidentally hit against something
stop something from having any effect
cause a disagreement or argument between people
move to a particular place at the same time as a lot of other people
if two or more things go together, they frequently exist together; if two things go together, they seem good, natural or attractive in combination with each other
come together with someone, either unexpectedly or as planned
talk more about your personal feelings and experiences; open a locked door, container or building; make it easier to travel or do business in a country
keep treating someone badly or unfairly, especially by criticising them
try to find out someone's opinions, ideas, feelings, etc by talking to them
look or behave like an older relative
begin to like someone or something; start doing something as a habit

idioms.idiom: be born with a silver spoon in your mouth
idioms.meaning: have advantages because you come from a rich family
understand the way another person thinks because you often have the same ideas and opinions they do

idioms.idiom: get on like a house on fire
idioms.meaning: become good friends very quickly and have a lot to talk to each other about
used for saying that someone is annoyed/pleased with you

idioms.idiom: like two peas in a pod
idioms.meaning: used for saying that two people look, behave or think exactly the same
have a good relationship with someone
provide a sensible way of judging how good, bad, important, etc something is in comparison with other things
used for saying someone has been weak in the ways most people are weak and should not be blamed for their behaviour
agree with someone or have the same opinion as them

J.title: Write one word in each gap.
J.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
J.passage:
The usual western idea of marriage is that you meet someone, (1) ...... in love, and then decide to marry each other. For those born (2) ...... some cultures, however, marriage may have quite a different meaning. In an arranged marriage, it is the parents who choose the partner and it's possible (3) ...... the young person getting married not to meet their future bride or groom (4) ...... person until the day of the wedding. Indeed, a young person might be promised to another (5) ...... a child, many years before the wedding day.

Amongst communities that practise arranged marriage, it's not unusual for a generation (6) ...... to open up between parents and children, with many young people refusing to accept an arranged marriage. However, if the partners treat each other (7) ...... respect and recognise that they are both (8) ...... equal value, there doesn't seem to be any real barrier to having a happy married life.

N.instructions: Use the word given in capitals to form a word that fits in the space.
N.passage: Very little in our lives prepares us for (1) ...... (PARENT). Suddenly, your life is turned upside down and all sorts of (2) ...... (FAMILIAR) demands are placed on you. (OBSERVE) of how our parents responded to us creates a model of parenting that is (6) ...... (INTIMATE) connected to the kind of parents we become. It's not uncommon for people to show the same child-rearing (7) ...... (CHARACTER) as their own parents. If your father was an (8) ......
